title: When Is My Baby Due? Due Date Calculator + FAQ 2026 | Hacé Cuentas

intro: The first question after a positive pregnancy test: **"When is my baby due?"** — answered here in 5 seconds. Enter the first day of your **last menstrual period (LMP)** (or a synthetic LMP if you conceived via IVF) and this calculator returns the estimated due date (EDD), current gestational age, and a full **FAQ for non-textbook scenarios** — irregular cycles, late ovulation, post-pill conception and more.
keyTakeaway: Your baby is most likely due **280 days after the first day of your last period** (Naegele's rule). But only **~5%** of babies arrive exactly on the EDD — **~80%** arrive between **week 37 and week 41**, usually around **week 39–40**. If your cycles aren't 28 days, read the "non-textbook" section below before trusting the LMP date.
useCases: You just got a positive test and you're asking yourself: "wait, when is this baby due?"
You're telling family/work and need a concrete date.
You had an IVF embryo transfer and need the equivalent LMP to use the calculator.
Your periods are irregular or you recently came off birth control — you need realistic expectations.
You're planning maternity leave and need a start date to back-calculate from.

fields.label: First day of your last period (LMP)
fields.help: If you don't know your LMP: for IVF transfer, use transfer date minus 19 days (5-day blastocyst) or minus 17 days (3-day embryo). For known conception date, use conception date minus 14 days.

dataUpdate.source: ACOG + Naegele's rule + NICE guideline NG201 (antenatal care)
dataUpdate.notes: The calculator applies Naegele's rule. The FAQ covers non-standard scenarios (IVF, irregular cycles, late ovulation) based on ACOG and NICE antenatal care guidance.

example.title: Example: Natural conception, LMP was February 1, 2026
steps: **Today** is April 22 → 80 days pregnant = **11 weeks + 3 days**.
example.result: Your baby is most likely due around **November 8, 2026**. You're currently **11 weeks + 3 days** pregnant.

explanation:
## The simple answer

Your baby is due approximately **40 weeks (280 days)** after the **first day of your last menstrual period**. This is called **Naegele's rule**, the worldwide standard.

But "due date" is misleading. Only **5% of babies** actually arrive on that exact day. A **normal full-term pregnancy** is anywhere between **37 and 42 weeks**, with the median around **39 weeks + 5 days** for first-time moms and **39 weeks + 0 days** for subsequent pregnancies.

## Non-textbook scenarios

### 1. Cycles that aren't 28 days

Naegele's rule assumes a **28-day cycle with ovulation on day 14**. If your cycle is longer (35 days, typical in PCOS), ovulation happens later — around day 21 — so **real gestational age is shorter** than the LMP suggests by ~1 week. In that case the baby arrives closer to **LMP + 287 days**.

Fix: ask for a **first-trimester dating ultrasound** (weeks 6–12). It measures the embryo's crown-rump length (CRL) directly and is accurate to ±5 days — much better than LMP for irregular cycles.

### 2. Conception right after coming off the Pill

It can take **1 to 3 cycles** for ovulation to normalize after stopping hormonal contraception. If you conceived in cycle 1, LMP-based dating is often off by a week. Again: **dating scan resolves it**.

### 3. IVF / assisted reproduction

Here the math is easier because the **transfer date is known exactly**. Compute a synthetic LMP:

- **Day-5 blastocyst transfer**: LMP equivalent = transfer date − 19 days (14 for ovulation + 5 for embryo age).
- **Day-3 embryo transfer**: LMP equivalent = transfer date − 17 days.
- **Frozen embryo transfer (FET)**: same as above, using the transfer date.

Enter that synthetic LMP in the calculator and you'll get an EDD accurate to ±1 day.

### 4. You know the exact conception date

If you're sure about the date of conception (charted ovulation, one-time intercourse window, etc.), the formula is:

> **EDD = conception date + 266 days**

Or equivalently, enter a synthetic LMP **14 days before** conception into the calculator.

### 5. You don't remember your LMP

**Very common**. Light spotting from implantation can be mistaken for a period, or you may just not track. If that's you:

- If under 12 weeks: dating ultrasound is almost certainly on your chart already — use that EDD.
- If ≥ 12 weeks: a growth ultrasound will estimate GA within ±10 days (less accurate than T1, but usable).

## "Which week am I most likely to go into labor?"

Real-world distribution of spontaneous onset (first-time moms):

| Week | % of deliveries |
|---|---|
| <37 (preterm) | ~11% |
| 37 | ~6% |
| 38 | ~18% |
| 39 | ~26% |
| 40 | ~19% |
| 41 | ~14% |
| 42+ | ~6% |

So **week 39** is the median. Subsequent pregnancies tend to be ~2–3 days earlier.

## How obstetricians revise the due date

**ACOG** recommends switching from LMP-based to ultrasound-based EDD if the difference exceeds:

| Ultrasound window | Redate if off by |
|---|---|
| ≤ 8 6/7 weeks | 5 days |
| 9 0/7 – 15 6/7 weeks | 7 days |
| 16 0/7 – 21 6/7 weeks | 10 days |
| 22 0/7 – 27 6/7 weeks | 14 days |
| ≥ 28 0/7 weeks | 21 days |

After redating, the new EDD is official for the rest of the pregnancy — it won't keep shifting with each scan.

## What does "due date" actually mean for planning?

For work and logistics, treat the EDD as a **midpoint** with a **±2 week window**. Practical guidance:

- **Start maternity leave 2–3 weeks before EDD** if you can — last-trimester fatigue is real.
- **Hospital bag packed at 34–36 weeks** — up to 11% of deliveries happen preterm.
- **Don't buy size-specific baby clothes** before 36 weeks if you don't know the baby's weight trajectory.
- **Tell work you'll be out 2 weeks before the EDD through "~8 weeks after"** and adjust on actual delivery.

## Sources

- [ACOG Committee Opinion 700](https://www.acog.org/clinical/clinical-guidance/committee-opinion/articles/2017/05/methods-for-estimating-the-due-date)
- [NICE guideline NG201 — Antenatal care](https://www.nice.org.uk/guidance/ng201)
- [WHO — Maternal health](https://www.who.int/health-topics/maternal-health)
- Jukic et al. (2013). *Length of human pregnancy and contributors to its natural variation*. Human Reproduction.

faq.q: When will my baby actually arrive?
faq.a: Most likely between **week 39 and week 40** of pregnancy. Only ~5% of babies arrive exactly on the due date. Around **80% arrive between week 37 and 41**. Preterm (<37) happens in ~11% of pregnancies.

faq.q: How do I know when my baby is due if I don't remember my last period?
faq.a: Ask for a **first-trimester ultrasound**. It measures the embryo's crown-rump length and gives an EDD accurate to ±5 days. This is the most reliable method even when LMP is known, and ACOG recommends using it to redate whenever LMP and ultrasound disagree significantly.

faq.q: How do I calculate the due date after IVF?
faq.a: Use the **transfer date**. For a day-5 blastocyst transfer, EDD = transfer date + 261 days (266 from conception − 5 days embryo age). For a day-3 embryo, EDD = transfer date + 263 days. Or enter a synthetic LMP into the calculator: transfer date − 19 (day-5) or −17 (day-3).

faq.q: I have irregular cycles. Is the LMP-based due date accurate?
faq.a: **No, it may be off by 1–2 weeks** if your cycles are longer than 28 days or variable. Ovulation in a 35-day cycle happens around day 21, not day 14, so the LMP formula **underestimates EDD**. Always confirm with a first-trimester dating ultrasound.

faq.q: I just stopped the Pill and got pregnant — is my due date reliable?
faq.a: It depends on how long after stopping you conceived. **Cycle 1 is often unpredictable** (delayed ovulation). If you conceived in cycles 2 or 3 with a clear LMP, the estimate is usually reliable. Either way, a first-trimester scan will confirm within ±5 days.

faq.q: How long is a normal pregnancy?
faq.a: **40 weeks from LMP** (~38 weeks from conception). Normal full-term range is **37–42 weeks**. Less than 37 is "preterm"; beyond 42 is "post-term" and most providers will recommend induction at 41 weeks to avoid complications.

faq.q: Can I guess the due date from the conception date?
faq.a: Yes. **EDD = conception date + 266 days**. That's equivalent to LMP + 280, since conception typically happens 2 weeks after LMP in a 28-day cycle.

faq.q: Why do first babies come later than second/third?
faq.a: Statistically, first-time moms deliver about **3–5 days later** than women on their second or subsequent pregnancy. Likely reasons include cervical readiness and uterine receptivity having "practiced" the first time. The effect is real but small — expect a few extra days of waiting if it's your first.

faq.q: Will my baby be born on the due date?
faq.a: Probably not. **Only about 5% of babies** are born exactly on the estimated due date. Think of the EDD as a **target week**, not a specific calendar day. Plan with ±2 weeks of flexibility.

sources.name: ACOG Committee Opinion 700 — Methods for Estimating the Due Date
Jukic et al. (2013) — Length of human pregnancy

howToSteps.name: Enter your LMP (or synthetic LMP for IVF)
howToSteps.text: For natural conception, use the first day of your last period. For IVF day-5, use transfer date minus 19 days.

howToSteps.name: Read your baby's estimated due date
howToSteps.text: The primary result is the date your baby is most likely to arrive (LMP + 280 days).

howToSteps.name: Check if your scenario needs adjustment
howToSteps.text: If you have irregular cycles, came off the Pill recently, or just had IVF, read the FAQ — those cases need a dating ultrasound for accuracy.

howToSteps.name: Plan with a ±2 week window
howToSteps.text: The due date is a midpoint. Most babies arrive within 2 weeks on either side. Pack your hospital bag by week 34–36.
